title: Judging Progress by Eye
concept: In practice, GAN progress is often judged by periodically looking at the generated samples themselves, not just watching loss numbers — a very different debugging workflow than earlier units relied on.

exercises.prompt: Recap: why can't you reliably judge GAN progress just by watching whether the loss numbers are dropping?

options.text: Because the generator's and discriminator's losses react to each other, so the curves can bounce even during real progress
Because GANs don't compute loss numbers at all
Because loss numbers are only shown after training finishes

exercises.explain: Right. So if the numbers alone are unreliable, what do practitioners actually check instead? That's today's lesson.

cards.title: Pull the print, don't just trust the timer
cards.body: In a darkroom, a photographer doesn't just set a timer and walk away — they periodically pull the print out and look at it under the safelight to see how the image is actually developing. GAN training works the same way: numbers alone won't tell you what the images actually look like.

cards.title: Sampling instead of scoring
cards.body: Because loss curves are noisy and don't reliably map to visual quality, practitioners regularly generate a batch of sample images during training and simply look at them — checking for realism, variety, and obvious defects — instead of relying only on the loss numbers.

cards.title: The darkroom check
cards.body: A photographer developing film doesn't trust the developing timer alone; they pull the print early, hold it to the light, and judge with their own eyes whether it needs more time or is already ruined. A GAN practitioner does the same thing with generated images: periodically pull a batch, look at it directly, and let your eyes catch things the loss number can't describe.

cards.title: What eyeballing catches that numbers miss
cards.body: A quick visual check can catch things a loss number would never flag on its own: all the generated faces starting to look suspiciously similar, weird artifacts creeping into every image, or a background always coming out warped. A dropping loss curve wouldn't specifically flag any of these — but a two-second glance at a sample grid would.

cards.title: "Isn't eyeballing samples just an unscientific shortcut?"
cards.body: It's tempting to think a real engineer would only trust hard numbers, and that looking at images is just a lazy substitute. In GAN training, it's the opposite: visual inspection is a standard, deliberate part of the workflow, precisely because the loss numbers are known to be unreliable narrators of what the images actually look like.

left.text:
Works well for classifiers
One number tells the story
Rarely need to 'look'

right.text:
Loss numbers are noisy
Periodically inspect sample grids
Trust your eyes too

exercises.prompt: Why do GAN practitioners regularly look at generated sample images instead of relying only on loss curves?

options.text: Loss numbers are noisy and don't reliably reflect visual quality, so eyeballing samples catches problems the numbers miss
Looking at images is required by law for AI training
Loss curves cannot be computed for GANs at all
Sample images replace the need for a discriminator

exercises.explain: Right — visual inspection catches what noisy loss numbers can't reliably describe.

exercises.prompt: True or false: in GAN training, periodically inspecting generated sample images is considered an unscientific shortcut that skilled practitioners avoid.
exercises.explain: False — it's a standard, deliberate part of the GAN training workflow, precisely because loss numbers are known to be unreliable on their own.

exercises.sentence: Like a photographer pulling a print to check it under the safelight instead of trusting the timer alone, GAN practitioners periodically ___ a batch of generated samples instead of trusting loss numbers alone.
exercises.explain: The habit is direct visual inspection — actually looking at what the generator is producing.

exercises.prompt: Which problem would a quick visual check of sample images likely catch that a dropping loss curve would NOT specifically flag?

options.text: Every generated face starting to look suspiciously similar to the others
The overall loss value increasing from 1.0 to 1.5
The training script throwing an error and stopping

exercises.explain: A loss number has no way to describe 'these all look the same' — only a look at the actual images reveals that.

exercises.prompt: You've now seen that GAN progress is judged both by (unreliable) loss numbers and by directly looking at samples. Given how tricky both signals can be, what's a natural next question?

options.text: What are the specific ways GAN training tends to go wrong, so you know what to actually look for?
How do you delete the discriminator to make training easier?
How do you convert generated images back into loss numbers?
Should you stop looking at loss numbers completely and never check them again?

exercises.explain: Exactly — next up, the specific failure modes to watch for.
